New York nursing homes fell below the state’s 3.50-hour staffing minimum on nearly half of days in 2025.

Eric Goldwein, MPH · June 2026

101,779 Days 2025 days below 3.50 HPRDDays < 3.50 HPRD

01 · Why this matters

Nearly half of NY facility-days fell below 3.50 HPRD

MethodsHPRD metricThreshold

New York state law requires nursing homes to provide at least 3.50 hours of care per resident per day. Though staffing is often summarized as a quarterly average, this analysis uses federal Payroll-Based Journal (PBJ) data—one record per nursing home per calendar day—to compare daily staffing levels against that standard.

In 2025, New York nursing homes fell below the 3.50 HPRD minimum on more than 100,000 facility-days — 47% of all daily records analyzed. Weekend shortfalls were more common, with 77% of Saturday and Sunday records falling below the threshold. Shortfalls were also more frequent among for-profit facilities and varied sharply by region, with the Bronx region showing the highest rate. These findings have implications for workforce planning, enforcement, resident advocacy, and how policymakers, families, and the public interpret New York nursing home staffing data.

Threshold note: Primary benchmark 3.50 HPRD (NY statutory total). MACPAC lists 3.56 HPRD when including a separate 0.06 full-time DON RN component. Descriptive PBJ mapping only—not NY DOH enforcement or legal advice.

Methods

Data: CMS PBJ daily staffing, CY2025 (NY only). Unit: facility-day (one home, one calendar day, census > 0).

HPRD: Total nursing hours (all PBJ nursing roles) ÷ census. Below minimum = strictly under the threshold (default 3.50; MACPAC 3.56 for NY total nursing, incl. 0.06 full-time DON RN).

NY role floors (informative): Also 2.20 CNA and 1.10 licensed nurse HPRD the same day; PBJ mapping only—not DOH enforcement.

Charts: HPRD slider recalculates day-of-week, provider, ownership, and county curves; role floors and holidays are build-time.

Limits: Self-reported PBJ; daily totals only; descriptive—not legal advice or a violation finding.

Full methodology & sources

New York Nursing Home Minimum Staffing

Nursing Homes are required by New York State Public Health Law and Regulations to maintain minimum staffing standards. These minimum standards require every nursing home to maintain daily staffing hours equal to 3.5 hours of care per resident per day by a certified nurse aide, licensed practical nurse, or registered nurse.

Of the 3.5 hours required, at least 2.2 hours of care per resident per day must be provided by a certified nurse aide (CNA) and at least 1.1 hours of care per resident per day must be provided by a licensed nurse.

Compliance with the minimum nursing staff requirements is determined on a quarterly basis using the most recent data available from the federal Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) Payroll Based Journal (PBJ). Nursing homes that fail to adhere to the minimum staffing standards are subject to civil penalties.

Penalties: DOH may impose a civil penalty of up to $2,000 per day for each day in a quarter that a facility fails to meet minimum nursing staff requirements, subject to mitigating factors (extraordinary circumstances, acute labor shortage in a region, or verifiable union dispute).

Source: NY Department of Health — Nursing Home Minimum Staffing
02 · Day of week

Any given Sunday: nearly four in five facility-days fell below 3.50 HPRD

MethodsDay of weekWeekends & holidays

Below-minimum staffing was far more common on weekends: 79.2% of Sunday facility-days fell below the 3.50 HPRD threshold, compared with 29.7% on Wednesday.

03 · Provider shortfalls

Some homes are below minimum almost every day

MethodsFacility tableProvider-day bands

Among 596 New York nursing homes in 2025, 34 were below 3.50 HPRD on every reported day and 95 on at least 90% of days; the remainder fall across the day-below bands below.

Note: The table and band chart show daily staffing reported below 3.50 HPRD, which differs from quarterly averages. In Q4 2025, 49% of facilities (290 of 589) averaged below 3.50 HPRD on quarterly PBJ totals.

04 · Ownership

For-profit facilities had higher daily shortfall rates

MethodsOwnership slices

Below-minimum rates differed sharply by ownership type. Statewide, for-profit nursing homes were below New York’s 3.50 HPRD minimum on 55.1% of 2025 facility-days, compared with 30.6% for non-profit homes and 20.5% for government-operated homes.

The same pattern appears within New York City. NYC for-profit homes were below minimum on 60.1% of facility-days, compared with 48.1% for NYC non-profit homes and 5.4% for NYC government-operated homes.

NYC government-operated homes represent a much smaller facility-day denominator than NYC for-profit homes.

For-profit facilities had higher daily shortfall rates Share of 2025 facility-days below New York’s 3.50 HPRD minimum2025 days < 3.50 HPRD · NY

NYC accounts for 168 of 596 New York nursing homes (28%) and 33.22% of the state’s recorded days below the 3.50 HPRD threshold. Source: CMS PBJ; analysis by PBJ320. Source: CMS PBJ · PBJ320 analysis

Ownership coverage: For-profit, non-profit, and government slices exclude 442 facility-days (109 reported below 3.50 HPRD) where CMS Provider Info (May 2026) lists ownership other than these three categories. All NY and NYC totals include every classified facility-day in the analysis.

06 · NY role floors

NY role floors (informative)

MethodsRole-floor mapping (AND rule)

New York’s minimum staffing rule has three parts: 3.50 total HPRD, including at least 2.20 CNA HPRD and at least 1.10 licensed nurse HPRD. A facility must meet all three thresholds to be fully compliant. This analysis maps federal PBJ staffing data to those thresholds for informational purposes only; it is not a formal NY DOH enforcement determination. Under the state rule, civil penalties of up to $2,000 per day may apply for each day in a quarter that a facility is out of compliance.

39.7% Days
Met all three NY floors (3.50 total + 2.20 CNA + 1.10 licensed)
54.9% Days
Below 2.20 CNA HPRD — most common single miss
Official NY Department of Health rule text, quarterly compliance process, and penalty guidance.
06 · By month

Little month-to-month change—the pattern is the day of the week

Methods: Months, weeks & holidays

Monthly rates stayed in a narrow band—about 44% (April) to 50% (December)—far smaller than the weekday–weekend gap above. December is the high month: it includes Christmas (a federal holiday) and typical year-end weekend patterns. Statewide, facility-days on federal holidays were about 52% below 3.50 HPRD vs. 47% on other days. Use By week for detail.

% of facility-days below 3.5 HPRD · by month · NY statewide · 2025% days < 3.5 HPRD · by month · NY · 2025

Dashed line: 50%. Monthly variation is small relative to day-of-week. Source: CMS PBJ; analysis by PBJ320. Source: CMS PBJ · PBJ320 analysis

~6 pp

April ~44%, December ~50%. December includes Christmas and more weekend-heavy weeks; holidays statewide run slightly higher than non-holiday days.

07 · Methods & sources

Methodology, data sources, and limitations

Data sources and scope

This analysis uses CMS Payroll-Based Journal (PBJ) daily nurse staffing for calendar year 2025: four quarterly CSV releases (CY2025Q1–Q4). Each record is one nursing home on one work date with role-level hours and census.

Facilities are limited to New York State CCNs present in PBJ during 2025. The unit of analysis is the facility-day (one home, one calendar day, census > 0).

Daily, not shift-level: PBJ publishes one staffing total per role per calendar day, not separate day/evening/night shifts. Nursing homes often run leaner overnight and heavier on dayside; a facility-day can meet a daily HPRD benchmark while averaging lower coverage over part of the 24-hour period. This report cannot detect within-day or shift-level variation.

Staffing metric (HPRD)

Total nursing HPRD: (RN + LPN + CNA + Med Aide + NA trainee + RN DON + RN admin + LPN admin) ÷ MDS census, aligned with PBJ320 facility dashboard logic.

NY statutory role floors (informative): CNA-side HPRD = CNA + Med Aide + NA trainee hours ÷ census (floor 2.20). LPN/RN-side HPRD = RN + LPN + RN DON + RN admin + LPN admin ÷ census (floor 1.10). Primary charts use total nursing HPRD (all roles, default benchmark 3.50). See NY role floors for counts below 2.20 / 1.10.

Threshold and “below minimum”

Primary threshold: 3.50 HPRD (report default). MACPAC’s state policy table lists 3.56 HPRD for New York total nursing, including 0.06 HPRD of a registered professional nurse to serve as the DON on a full-time basis.

A facility-day counts as below when HPRD is strictly less than the threshold (days exactly at the threshold count as compliant).

The Toggle HPRD threshold control (day-of-week section) recalculates hero KPIs, day-of-week bars, provider compliance (histogram and facility table), ownership chart and table, and county map and top-county bars. Static sections (role-floor counts, holiday callouts) stay at build-time values.

Ownership, geography, and charts

Ownership type and county come from CMS Provider Info (normalized snapshot, May 2026), joined to PBJ by CCN.

NYC = Kings (Brooklyn), Queens, Bronx, New York (Manhattan), Richmond (Staten Island).

Weekend views combine Saturday and Sunday facility-days before aggregating.

Grouped ownership bars and the slice table use the same HPRD threshold as the toggle above. For-profit, non-profit, and government rows exclude facility-days where Provider Info lists ownership outside those three categories (442 days statewide at 3.50 HPRD in this build; 109 below minimum).

Day-of-week chart

One bar per weekday (Mon–Sun) for NY statewide facility-days with census > 0. Below minimum at threshold T: share of that weekday’s facility-days with total nursing HPRD strictly below T. Values come from precomputed day-of-week curves and update when you move the HPRD toggle.

County map and top-county bars

County map uses approximate county centroids (not facility address geocoding). Bubble area scales with facility-day volume; fill color encodes % below minimum at the active threshold (green / amber / red tiers in legend). The horizontal county chart lists the top 10 counties by volume; bar fill uses a blue scale by % below (see chart note).

Facility table

Each row is one nursing home (CCN) with 2025 facility-day count, mean HPRD, and a precomputed below-minimum curve for thresholds 2.50–5.50 HPRD. Sorting and filters run in your browser; changing the HPRD toggle swaps the curve for each home—no live download on each click.

Provider-day bands

For each home, compute the share of its 2025 facility-days (census > 0) with total nursing HPRD strictly below the active threshold. Sort homes into bands: 100% of days below, 90–99%, 75–89%, and so on. Bars show the percent of nursing homes in each band (596 in this report). Same facility curves as the table; recomputed when the HPRD toggle changes.

NY role floors (informative)

For each facility-day with census > 0, three HPRD measures are computed from PBJ role hours:

Total nursing HPRD
All nursing roles in the default metric ÷ census. Compared to floor 3.50 in this report.
CNA-side HPRD
CNA + Med Aide + NA trainee ÷ census. Floor 2.20 (NY statute).
LPN/RN-side HPRD
RN + LPN + RN DON + RN admin + LPN admin ÷ census. Floor 1.10 (NY statute).

Met all three — the day meets total, CNA-side, and LPN/RN-side floors together (AND). Below 2.20 CNA-side — CNA-side HPRD is strictly under 2.20 (a facility-day can miss CNA-side but not total HPRD, or the reverse).

Facility-day counts and shares appear in NY role floors (denominator 216,134, same as statewide charts). Descriptive PBJ mapping only—not NY DOH quarterly enforcement.

Months, weeks, and holidays

Monthly below-minimum rates move in a narrow band compared with day-of-week. Small month-to-month drift largely reflects how many Saturdays and Sundays fall in each month.

Monthly and weekly line charts aggregate facility-days by calendar month or ISO week at the active threshold.

Federal holidays are not labeled in PBJ; we tag U.S. federal holiday dates from the calendar when building the report. Holiday vs. non-holiday shares at 3.50 HPRD are fixed in the published page (not driven by the month/week slider). Main charts are not holiday-adjusted.

Limitations

  1. Based on facilities' self-reported PBJ daily data, not NY DOH enforcement records or penalty assessments.
  2. This report does not evaluate whether NY minimum staffing policy is sufficient, effective, or well designed—only how often PBJ-reported days fall below mapped thresholds.
  3. PBJ provides one total per role per calendar day, not shift-level (day/evening/night) hours.
  4. Federal holidays are mapped from the calendar, not from PBJ fields (see methodology).
  5. Total nursing HPRD includes RN DON, RN admin, and LPN admin.
  6. Days with zero census excluded. Days at exactly the threshold count as compliant (strictly below only).
  7. Ownership and county from CMS Provider Info, May 2026 snapshot. NYC = five boroughs.

Primary sources

  • CMS PBJ Daily Nurse Staffing
  • NY DOH — Nursing Home Minimum Staffing
  • N.Y. PHL § 2803-d
  • MACPAC State Staffing Policies

How to replicate this analysis

Any analyst with CMS public files can reproduce the logic without PBJ320 software.

  1. PBJ daily staffing — Download quarterly CMS PBJ nurse staffing files for your state and year from data.cms.gov (e.g. CY2025Q1–Q4).
  2. Provider Info — Download ownership and county from the Provider Data Catalog; join to PBJ by CCN.
  3. Facility-days — One row per home per calendar day (census > 0). Sum hours by role; divide by census for HPRD; flag days strictly below your threshold.
  4. Aggregates — Statewide and slice-level shares below minimum by day of week, ownership, county, and other cuts used in the charts.
  5. NY role floors — Compute CNA-side and LPN/RN-side HPRD; count days meeting all three statutory floors (AND) for informative sensitivity tables.
  6. Publish — Precompute threshold curves and facility-level summaries, then embed them in a static report page (as here) or serve the same structured data from an API.

Report prepared by PBJ320 · underlying tables generated 2026-06-03 · verification workbook available for download (CSV package).

This report is descriptive research on self-reported federal data. It is not legal advice and does not assert regulatory violation, negligence, or causation.
